news.source: ABC News & Headlines – Australian Broadcasting Corporation

news.title: Australia 31-33 Ireland: Nations Championship rugby union international – as it happened

sections.heading: UK residency offers transformational revenue
sections.body: Fiji continues its residency in the United Kingdom this week ahead of the trip to Murrayfield. Fiji Rugby chairman John Sanday described the move to play "home" fixtures in the northern hemisphere as a "transformational opportunity" for the union. The decision was driven by stadium capacity requirements and the potential to reinvest matchday revenues back into high-performance facilities and infrastructure in the South Pacific.

sections.heading: Recovery focus following England defeat
sections.body: The squad has shifted focus to recovery and conditioning in the United Kingdom after the physical toll of the Round Two loss at Hill Dickinson Stadium. No new injury concerns were reported in today's coverage following the heavy defeat to England. The coaching staff is prioritizing the squad's adaptation to the northern hemisphere climate as they prepare for the high tempo expected at Murrayfield.

sections.heading: Scotland prepares for significant rotation
sections.body: Scotland returns to Edinburgh following a 42-28 defeat to South Africa in Pretoria. Gregor Townsend has signaled "a lot of changes" to his matchday squad for the upcoming Test. The hosts will be without a senior Glasgow lock who has been ruled out for several weeks due to surgery, while their vice-captain is undergoing assessment after a failed head injury assessment against the Springboks.

sections.heading: Fiji remains bottom of the log
sections.body: Fiji sits 12 of 12 in the standings after consecutive losses to open the Nations Championship. Following the 8-73 defeat to England, the Flying Fijians go into the weekend looking for their first points of the tournament. A result in Edinburgh is critical to lift the side off the bottom of the log before the competition shifts away from their temporary northern hemisphere base.

sections.heading: Quirke named on Monday as Pollock pressure builds
sections.body: Steve Borthwick named Raffi Quirke on Monday to replace the injured Alex Mitchell following the 73-8 win over Fiji. While Henry Pollock’s hat-trick has created intense selection pressure, the head coach is expected to maintain consistency in his midfield. Borthwick must now decide if the side that produced 11 tries in Liverpool can replicate that dominance against a Pumas side that has recently beaten New Zealand.

sections.heading: Mitchell ruled out of Argentina trip
sections.body: Alex Mitchell has been ruled out of Saturday’s Test against Argentina after sustaining a hamstring injury during the 11-try win in Liverpool. The scrum-half is expected to miss the remainder of this opening block, a significant blow given his role in the 73-8 demolition of Fiji. No other fresh concerns were reported today, with the squad focusing on recovery and conditioning before the long flight south.

sections.heading: Fortress Santiago awaits in Round 3
sections.body: Argentina enter Round 3 with momentum after a 35-21 victory over Wales, rebounding from an opening-round loss to Scotland. Los Pumas return to Estadio Único Madre de Ciudades, a venue where they have never lost and recently claimed the scalp of the world champion Springboks. With the local atmosphere expected to be febrile, England must overcome a side that has taken several notable scalps recently.

sections.heading: England chase top-half finish for first leg
sections.body: England go into the weekend seventh in the standings following their split of the opening two rounds. This fixture marks the final match of the first leg before the competition pauses until November. A win in South America would secure a positive record for the summer and potentially propel Borthwick’s side into the top half of the log, depending on results in the other cross-hemisphere fixtures.

match.venue: Estadio Único Madre de Ciudades, Santiago del Estero

sections.heading: Selection focus shifts to backline vacancies
sections.body: Fabien Galthié must navigate the trip to Japan without one of his premier finishers. With the squad still adjusting after their win over Australia, selection focus remains on the backline composition. Today's coverage indicates no formal team sheet has been released yet, but the coaching staff is evaluating options to replace the scoring threat lost earlier this month.

sections.heading: Penaud remains sidelined with calf injury
sections.body: Damian Penaud is officially ruled out of Saturday's Test against Japan. The winger sustained a calf injury during the opening-round loss and has not recovered in time for the final leg of the July window. Beyond this confirmed absence, there is no fresh injury news in today's coverage regarding the players who featured in the Round 2 victory over Australia.

sections.heading: Japan seek redemption after previous demolition
sections.body: Japan enter this fixture looking to erase the memory of a 52-12 defeat against France in their last encounter. During that match in Paris, the French side crossed for eight tries in an emphatic performance. While the Brave Blossoms are known for their fearlessness and ability to surprise favorites, they must tighten a defense that struggled to contain France's power.

sections.heading: France chase podium spot in Round 3
sections.body: France go into the weekend sitting four of 12 on the log after splitting their first two fixtures. Following a 16-point victory over Australia, a second consecutive win is essential to keep pressure on the tournament leaders. A bonus-point result in Japan could see the team climb into the top three depending on other results across the final round of the window.

sections.heading: Gibson-Park return key to Auckland plan
sections.body: Andy Farrell is expected to restore his front-line starters for the trip to Auckland following heavy rotation for the 36-20 victory over Japan. New Zealand-born scrum-half Jamison Gibson-Park is the focal point of the All Blacks' defensive planning after being rested last weekend. Dave Rennie noted that the Leinster half-back is a "big part of their success," tasking his side with limiting his influence.

sections.heading: Baloucoune expected to bolster backline options
sections.body: Andy Farrell expressed optimism regarding Robert Baloucoune, who is expected to be available for selection after resting a tight hamstring for a second consecutive week. The winger's return would bolster a squad where 33 of the 36 players have already seen match action this month. Aside from managing the wider group's recovery, there are no fresh injury concerns reported in today’s coverage from camp.

sections.heading: All Blacks defend formidable Eden Park record
sections.body: New Zealand returns to their Eden Park citadel, a venue where they have not lost a Test match since 1994. Dave Rennie’s side carries significant momentum into the weekend following a 47-17 victory over Italy, a match in which Will Jordan became the country’s all-time leading try-scorer. Rennie warned his players against complacency, acknowledging that Ireland won the series the last time they toured.

sections.heading: Unbeaten sides contest top log positions
sections.body: Ireland enters the weekend third in the Nations Championship log, one of several sides maintaining a perfect record after two rounds. A win in Auckland would secure a third consecutive victory and potentially move Andy Farrell’s side to the top of the standings. Facing a New Zealand team that is also undefeated — Ireland is aiming to end a 52-game unbeaten run at the venue.

sections.heading: Azzurri look to repeat Udine heroics
sections.body: Italy prepares for the trip to Perth buoyed by recent success against the Wallabies. The 26-19 victory in Udine last November showcased the impact of backs with Australian heritage, as Louis Lynagh and Monty Ioane both crossed for tries. Following a competitive showing during the loss to New Zealand, the Azzurri are looking to replicate that clinical edge to secure their first win of the campaign.

sections.heading: Coverage silent on fresh squad concerns
sections.body: No fresh injury reports appeared in today's coverage following the Round 2 defeat to the All Blacks. The lack of specific medical updates suggests the coaching staff may have most of the squad to choose from for the flight to Perth. Management is expected to prioritize recovery protocols before naming the match-day 23 later this week, following the physical toll of the opening two rounds.

sections.heading: Schmidt wary of improving Italy
sections.body: Australia enters the weekend in poor form, having lost nine of their past 10 Tests. Coach Joe Schmidt has reportedly called up another rookie playmaker after inexperienced halves struggled in their recent loss to France. However, the Wallabies could be bolstered by the return of Ben Donaldson. Schmidt remains wary of the Italians, noting they showed enough against New Zealand to put his side on edge.

sections.heading: Azzurri fighting to climb the log
sections.body: Italy goes into the weekend 11th in the standings after consecutive losses to start the tournament. While the Azzurri remain winless, a victory in Perth would ensure they stay clear of the bottom of the log. Securing a third-ever win over Australia is vital for momentum before the championship concludes, especially as the team looks to build on the progress shown in Udine last year.

sections.heading: Townsend flags sweeping changes for Murrayfield
sections.body: Gregor Townsend has signaled a significant overhaul for the upcoming clash at Murrayfield. Following the 42-28 defeat to South Africa, the head coach indicated there would be "a lot of changes" to the side as he manages his squad's workload. After the physical toll of playing at altitude in Pretoria — an experience former prop Peter Wright described as "horrible" — fresh legs are expected.

sections.heading: Glasgow lock sidelined as vice-captain enters protocols
sections.body: The pack faces a reshuffle after a Glasgow lock was ruled out for several weeks, requiring surgery to address an injury sustained in Round 2. Midfield options also remain under scrutiny as the vice-captain undergoes assessment following a failed Head Injury Assessment in Pretoria. The medical team is monitoring his progress through return-to-play protocols to determine if he can feature in the second home match of the campaign.

sections.heading: Fijian 'home' game moves to Edinburgh
sections.body: Fiji arrive in Edinburgh having adopted the United Kingdom as their base for the tournament's opening half. After a narrow loss to Wales in Cardiff, the Flying Fijians are seeking their first win of the campaign. Playing away from Suva allows the visitors to access higher match-day revenues, but they remain dangerous opponents intent on repeating their historical habit of upsetting Tier One nations.

sections.heading: Scotland chase top-half spot after Pretoria loss
sections.body: Scotland enters the weekend sitting sixth in the 12-team log after split results in the opening rounds. With a record of one win and one loss, securing a victory is essential to keep pace with the tournament leaders. Following the frustration of missed opportunities in South Africa, a clinical performance is required to improve the current negative points difference and climb the table.

sections.heading: Selection shuffle ahead of Durban
sections.body: Steve Tandy faces a significant reshuffle as Wales prepare for the world champions. Josh Adams has described this fixture as the "hardest test" of the current coaching regime’s progress. With the squad reeling from the defeat in Argentina, leadership changes are expected, and adjustments to the match-day 23 are set to be confirmed following the emergence of fresh injury concerns within the camp.

sections.heading: Lake set to miss out
sections.body: Dewi Lake is set to miss his first game under Steve Tandy, with the captain likely sidelined for Saturday's Test. The concern surrounding the hooker’s fitness has prompted the coaching staff to fly in an additional player as cover. No further details regarding the extent of the injury have been released in today's coverage, leaving the remainder of the squad's availability status unchanged.

sections.heading: Springboks test new talent
sections.body: South Africa have opted for rotation, naming 10 changes to their starting side. The Springboks have included four uncapped players in their XV, including 20-year-old fly-half Vuyo Moyo. Pieter-Steph du Toit will again captain the side as they look to maintain their position at the top of the southern hemisphere standings, leaving Wales to face an experimental but formidable world championship outfit.

sections.heading: Recovery mission in the standings
sections.body: Following the 21-35 loss to Argentina, Wales currently sit 8th of 12 in the log. With 1 win and 1 loss from their opening two fixtures, the team is looking to improve their position as they face the tournament leaders. A positive result in Durban is vital to building momentum and proving their competitive progress after the disappointment in San Juan.

sections.heading: Pumas build momentum toward England showdown
sections.body: Los Pumas enter their final Nations Championship first-leg fixture with renewed confidence following Saturday's 35-21 victory over Wales. After an opening-round defeat to Scotland, the squad has regained momentum and will be eager to maintain their form in front of a home crowd this weekend at Estadio Único Madre de Ciudades. The team continues its preparations as the competition nears its first-leg conclusion.

sections.heading: Medical updates unavailable
sections.body: There is no fresh injury news regarding the Argentina squad in today's coverage. The current reports provide no specific details on the physical status of the players following the weekend's win against Wales, focusing instead on the team's upcoming match-day preparations and the broader context of the tournament standings as the squad readies for England.

sections.heading: England shuffle squad after Mitchell injury
sections.body: England arrive following a 73-8 victory over Fiji, though they face a personnel setback with scrum-half Alex Mitchell ruled out due to a hamstring injury. Raffi Quirke has been called into the squad as his replacement. England currently hold a streak of 5 consecutive wins against Argentina, though their recent tournament form has seen them searching for their first win of this specific campaign.

sections.heading: Pushing for position on the log
sections.body: Argentina sits 5th in the 12-team Nations Championship log, holding a record of one win and one loss with a point difference of +5. With the competition reaching the final round of the first leg, a result this weekend is vital for climbing the table before the tournament resumes in November, as the team looks to build on their recent victory over Wales.

sections.heading: Donaldson pushing for Perth selection
sections.body: Ben Donaldson is in a race to prove his fitness for this weekend’s Test in Perth. The playmaker, who missed the loss to France in round two, has stated he feels on track to make his return. Joe Schmidt faces a significant selection decision at flyhalf for his final Test in charge, with the coach weighing up whether to recall a veteran for this must-win assignment.

sections.heading: Playmaker recovery remains the focus
sections.body: While Ben Donaldson aims to return from the injury that sidelined him for the France Test, there is no further information in today’s coverage regarding the wider squad’s availability. With the team preparing for their upcoming clash with Italy, the focus remains on rehabilitation and managing the fitness of key players to ensure the squad is prepared for the physical demands of the weekend’s fixture.

sections.heading: Italy momentum causes concern
sections.body: The Italians enter this match buoyed by their performance in a competitive loss to the All Blacks last Saturday. Having secured notable victories over Australia in recent years, including a 26-19 win last November, the visitors have shown they possess the tactical depth to challenge the Wallabies. Schmidt is reportedly on edge as he prepares to counter an Italian side that has become an increasingly difficult opponent.

sections.heading: Search for first championship points
sections.body: Australia sits 10th on the log following consecutive defeats to start the campaign. With two losses and a points difference of -18, this round three fixture is vital for the side to register their first points of the Nations Championship. The Wallabies are currently in a difficult run, having lost nine of their past 10 Tests, adding significant pressure to the outcome in Western Australia.

sections.heading: Looking for a response after Ireland defeat
sections.body: Japan enter the week searching for improvements following their 20-36 defeat against Ireland last Saturday. Despite tries from Taira and Hayate Era, and accurate kicking from Matsunaga, the side struggled to contain the visitors. Coaches will be working this week to address the performance gaps revealed in that fixture before the squad turns its full attention toward the upcoming challenge against France.

sections.heading: Conditioning focus in camp
sections.body: There is no fresh injury news in today's coverage following the weekend's match against Ireland. The team is currently focused on recovery and conditioning sessions as they prepare for the next round. Management will continue to monitor the squad's physical status throughout the week to ensure all players are available for the selection process ahead of the game on Saturday.

sections.heading: France face Japan without Penaud
sections.body: France travel to face Japan after securing a 42-26 victory over Australia last weekend. The French side, who remain a significant threat, will be without winger Damian Penaud for the remainder of the tournament. Penaud was ruled out of the remaining Nations Championship matches after sustaining a calf injury during their opening tournament game, creating a notable vacancy in the French backline.

sections.heading: Mid-table pressure for Japan
sections.body: Japan currently sit ninth in the 12-team log after two rounds. With one win and one loss, the team has accumulated a points difference of plus 1. Securing a result in the upcoming fixture against France is essential for Japan to climb the standings and maintain momentum, as every point earned in this tournament is vital for the final table position.

sections.heading: Preparation shifts toward high-stakes encounter
sections.body: Following the dominant 47-17 victory over Italy, the squad has transitioned focus toward the upcoming clash against Ireland. The team remains in high spirits after Will Jordan’s record-breaking hat-trick performance in Wellington. While the side has maintained strong momentum, management is currently evaluating the balance of the lineup required to challenge an Irish outfit that recently secured back-to-back bonus point victories.

sections.heading: Status of the squad
sections.body: There is no fresh injury news in today’s coverage following the conclusion of the second round. The team continues its preparation for the weekend without reports of new setbacks, and updates regarding individual player availability for the selection process are expected as the training week progresses.

sections.heading: Farrell prepares for Eden Park challenge
sections.body: Ireland arrives with confidence after a 36-20 win over Japan. Coach Andy Farrell noted that facing the All Blacks at their Eden Park citadel requires “the performance of our lives.” While Farrell acknowledged the intimidating nature of the venue, he stated, “We’re the lucky ones, we have a shot at doing something that nobody’s done.” Ireland will be looking to rectify lineout concerns identified during their Japan tour.

sections.heading: Maintaining momentum in the standings
sections.body: New Zealand currently sits second in the Nations Championship log with 2 wins from 2 matches, holding a points difference of +32. A victory this weekend is critical to keep pace with the competition leaders. Given the historical record at home, the team enters this fixture aiming to extend a 50-match unbeaten run at the venue and solidify their position at the top end of the table.

sections.heading: Four debutants handed starting roles
sections.body: South Africa have named four uncapped players in their starting XV for the clash against Wales in Durban. Among the new faces, 20-year-old Sharks fly-half Vusi Moyo is set to make his international debut. Rassie Erasmus expressed his confidence in the selection, stating he is “nice and calm” regarding how the debutants will perform during the third round of the Nations Championship.

sections.heading: Squad health update
sections.body: Today's coverage contains no information regarding individual player injuries or the squad's specific recovery status following the round two victory over Scotland. The reporting has remained focused on the selection of the debutant quartet and the tactical build-up toward the encounter in Durban.

sections.heading: Wales face captaincy challenge
sections.body: Steve Tandy’s side heads into this fixture following reports that captain Dewi Lake is set to miss the game. Wales have called up a replacement hooker to join the touring squad as they look for a positive reaction. Josh Adams has acknowledged the scale of the task, describing the match against the world champions as the toughest test of their progress under the current coaching regime.

sections.heading: Building on a perfect start
sections.body: South Africa currently sits atop the southern hemisphere standings with 2 wins from 2 matches. Maintaining this momentum is the clear priority, as the squad looks to build on the 87 points scored across the opening rounds. Securing a win in Durban would consolidate the team’s position at the top of the log before the competition reaches its halfway point.
